coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots confined in Y(OH)3 matrix with room temperature phosphorescence for information security applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Ping
Zheng, Yihao
He, Xiaoyan
Peng, Xiaoli
Huang, Weilan
Ying, Beichen
Zhang, Xuejie
Hu, Chaofan
Lei, Bingfu
Liu, Yingliang
Zhuang, Jianle
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot (CD)-based room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have aroused wide attention owing to their excellent photophysical properties. However, the reports of RTP CDs in information encryption and erasure applications are limited. Herein, a new-type of CD-based RTP material is constructed by incorporating the fluorescent CDs into yttrium hydroxide matrix (CDs@Y(OH)3). For the first time, the Y(OH)3 as a rigid matrix is used to stabilize the triplet state of the CDs and protect the CDs from oxygen. Furthermore, the intramolecular motions of the CDs are limited, due to the space confinement of the Y(OH)3 matrix and the formation of hydrogen bonds, thus effectively suppressing the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons and promoting phosphorescence emission. Based on superior optical performance, the CDs@Y(OH)3 composites are successfully applied in information encryption, and decryption. In addition, by destroying the Y(OH)3 matrix with acetic acid spray, the phosphorescence is invalidated and the information is erased after decryption. This finding not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of CD-based RTP materials but also reveals the great potential of CDs in information security and protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
CDs@Y(OH)3
Information security

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HR-TEM images of the CDs. (b) SEM images of the CDs@Y(OH)3 (the inset of Fig. 1b is an enlarged TEM image of CDs@Y(OH)3). (c) TEM and HR-TEM images of the CDs@Y(OH)3. (d) XRD patterns of CDs@Y(OH)3, Y(OH)3, CDs and the standard pattern of hexagonal Y(OH)3 (JCPDS No. 83–2042).

(a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs@Y(OH)3, Y(OH)3 and CDs. (b) XPS survey spectra of the CDs@Y(OH)3. High-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C 1s, (d) N 1s and (e) O 1s. (f) Schematic of the microstructure of CDs@Y(OH)3.

(a) Schematic diagram of the structures of Y(OH)3 and CDs, as well as the formation of hydrogen bonds. (b) Electrostatic potentials of the two structures: CDs and Y(OH)3. (c) Isosurface plot of the Intrinsic Gravitational Moment of Hybridization (IGMH) for CDs@Y(OH)3 (on the left), and scatter plot of the IGMH (on the right).

(a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence (FL) emission and excitation, phosphorescence (Phos) emission and excitation spectra of CDs@Y(OH)3. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra and (c) afterglow emission spectra of CDs@Y(OH)3 excited at different wavelengths. (d) Afterglow decay curve of the CDs@Y(OH)3 at 496 nm under excitation of 360 nm. (e) Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@Y(OH)3 at the low temperature (77 K) under excitation of 360 nm. (f) Afterglow spectra of CDs@Y(OH)3 at different temperatures under excitation of 360 nm.

(a) Photographs of an experiment to evaluate the stability of CDs@Y(OH)3 in different solutions. (b, d) Phosphorescent intensities and (c, e) lifetimes of CDs@Y(OH)3 in DMF, Ethanol, H2O, DMSO, Methanol, acetic acid, dilute HCl, H3PO4, dilute HNO3 and dilute H2SO4, respectively.
Schematic illustration of RTP mechanism of CDs@Y(OH)3.

(a) Model Illustration of information security based on CDs@Y(OH)3. (b) The images of “8888” made with CDs@Y(OH)3 and CDs-1@Y(OH)3 (use pure Y(OH)3 as the interference with only fluorescence) for information encryption.
Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure for CDs@Y(OH)3.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots confined in Y(OH)3 matrix with room temperature phosphorescence for information security applications

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Biobased Materials and Energy of Ministry of Education/Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Optical Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot (CD)-based room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials have aroused wide attention owing to their excellent photophysical properties. However, the reports of RTP CDs in information encryption and erasure applications are limited. Herein, a new-type of CD-based RTP material is constructed by incorporating the fluorescent CDs into yttrium hydroxide matrix (CDs@Y(OH)3). For the first time, the Y(OH)3 as a rigid matrix is used to stabilize the triplet state of the CDs and protect the CDs from oxygen. Furthermore, the intramolecular motions of the CDs are limited, due to the space confinement of the Y(OH)3 matrix and the formation of hydrogen bonds, thus effectively suppressing the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons and promoting phosphorescence emission. Based on superior optical performance, the CDs@Y(OH)3 composites are successfully applied in information encryption, and decryption. In addition, by destroying the Y(OH)3 matrix with acetic acid spray, the phosphorescence is invalidated and the information is erased after decryption. This finding not only provides a new strategy for the preparation of CD-based RTP materials but also reveals the great potential of CDs in information security and protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Optical information encryption, decryption and erasure are of great significance for information security and protection [1–4]. In recent years, the applications of room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials with long-lived excited states in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption have been widely discussed and reported. However, conventional inorganic and organometallic compounds have the disadvantages such as high cost and potential toxicity [5–9]. New pure organic RTP materials require a complicated and time-consuming synthesis process and also have a short lifetime and poor stability [10–14]. Therefore, developing low-cost and high-performance RTP materials with facile preparation process is extremely important.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of CD-based luminescent nanomaterials, has attracted wide attention due to its excellent optical properties, simple and green preparation method, and low toxicity [15–18]. Since electrons in the triplet state are easily quenched by mechanical motions (vibration, collision, and thermal motion, etc.) and environmental quenching agents (oxygen and water) [19,20]. Therefore, CDs usually only exhibit fluorescence at room temperature, and their phosphorescence is difficult to observe. To solve the problem mentioned above, a variety of strategies have been developed to realize the microstructure design and optical property regulation of CD-based RTP materials, which include introducing heteroatoms and heavy atoms, and developing new rigid matrices [21–24]. Especially, the matrix-assisted method is one of the most popular methods in recent years. The RTP of CDs has been obtained by embedding CDs into a variety of matrices such as polymer matrices (poly(vinyl alcohol [25], polyurethane [26], polyacrylic acid [27], polyacrylamide [28], etc.), inorganic crystals (NaCl [29], sulphates and oxalates [30], Y(OH)xF3-x [31], YF3 [32], layered hydroxides [20], etc.), porous materials (zeolites [33], silica [34], etc.) or others (urea/biuret [35,36], boric acid [37], etc.). When the CDs are embedded into the rigid matrices, the hydrogen, covalent or ionic bonds formed between CDs and the matrix can effectively fix the triplet excitons of CDs, resulting in efficient RTP emissions.

In this work, we adopted a novel nanotube-confined CDs strategy to obtain a new CD-based RTP material. To be specific, fluorescent CDs were prepared using citric acid and ethylenediamine, and then the CDs were added to the mixture of Y(NO3)3·6H2O and NaOH for a hydrothermal reaction to obtain CDs@Y(OH)3. To the best of our knowledge, this type of inorganic material was first found as a matrix for CD-based RTP material. The confinement effect provided by Y(OH)3 nanotube is primarily responsible for realizing the RTP of CDs. Finally, in view of the excellent performance of CDs@Y(OH)3 composites, we demonstrated their application in information security.

Reagent grade Y(NO3)3·6H2O, sodium hydroxide, citric acid and ethylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were used as received without further treatment. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the study.

CDs were prepared by a modified method [38]. 1.0507 g citric acid and 335 μL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 10 mL DI-water in a beaker and stirred for 30 min. Then the mixed solution was transferred to a 50 mL PPL-lined (black) stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting solution was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) to obtain CDs after freeze-drying.

Y(NO3)3·6H2O (0.2 M, 8 mL), CDs (0.01 mg/mL, 100 μL) aqueous solution were added to a beaker and stirred continuously. Then NaOH (0.5 M, 9.6 mL) aqueous solution was added and stirred for 30 min. The mixture solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted for 12 h at 180 °C. After being cooled down to room temperature, the precipitate was centrifuged and washed with DI water three times, and dried at 60 °C overnight to prepare CDs@Y(OH)3 (Scheme 1 ).

The preparation process of CDs@Y(OH)3 is shown in Scheme 1. Briefly, CDs (seed CDs were prepared in advance), Y(NO3)3 and NaOH mixture solutions were heated at 180 °C for 12 h in an autoclave. The obtained precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water, then dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain CDs@Y(OH)3. The morphology and structure of CDs and CDs@Y(OH)3 were further investigated. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the pure CDs appear as highly dispersed nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 3 nm (Fig. S1). Additionally, the high-resolution (HR)-TEM images (inset of Fig. 1a) show that the as-prepared CDs exhibit well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite. As shown in Fig. S2a and Fig. 1b, the pure Y(OH)3 and the CDs@Y(OH)3 exhibit similar well-dispersed nano-tubular morphology, which provide a large number of embedding sites for CDs. The well-resolved lattice fringes with 0.21 nm can be observed from the HR-TEM image of the CDs@Y(OH)3 (Fig. 1c), which can be ascribed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon. Fig. 1d demonstrates that the as-prepared matrix is hexagonal Y(OH)3. Compared with Y(OH)3, a higher elevation in the XRD pattern of CDs@Y(OH)3 at 20–45° can be attributed to the incorporation of amorphous CDs. The above discussions further confirm the successful combination of CDs with the Y(OH)3 matrix. In addition, the diameter of Y(OH)3 was measured to be 79 nm by the TEM image (Fig. S2b). Since the diameter of the CDs is much smaller than that of Y(OH)3, we deduce that CDs may be embedded either inside or on the surface of Y(OH)3.

To analyze the surface functional groups of the CDs@Y(OH)3, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and x-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were utilized. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs@Y(OH)3 possess abundant –OH, –NH2 and –COOH functional groups on their surface. The strong absorption peak located at 3617 cm−1 originates from –OH stretching vibration of Y(OH)3, and the absorption band at 3127∼3490 cm−1 comes from –OH/N–H stretching vibration of CDs. Moreover, other obvious characteristic peaks centered at 1619, 1378 and 1056 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO/CN, C–N, and C–O of the CDs, respectively. To further analyze the composition and functional groups of the CDs@Y(OH)3, the XPS spectra were gathered (Fig. 2b). The full survey XPS spectra of the CDs@Y(OH)3 mainly contains Y 3d (158.8 eV), C 1s (285.1 eV), N 1s (396.0 eV) and O 1s (531.3 eV) with atomic percentages of 33.14 %, 1.55 %, 47.91 %, and 14.60 %, respectively. More in detail, the HR-XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2c) reveals the presence of C–C/CC (284.8 eV), C–O/C–N (285.4 eV) and O–CO (289.4 eV). The N 1s spectrum is divided into three peaks located at 392.2 eV, 394.9 eV and 397.9 eV, indicating that the N element mainly exists in the form of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively (Fig. 2d). There are three characteristic peaks in the O 1s spectra at 530.7 eV, 531.3 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to CO, C–O/O–H and OC–O, respectively (Fig. 2e). The FT-IR and XPS results demonstrate the presence of a large number of N- and O-containing functional groups in CDs@Y(OH)3, further proving the incorporation of CDs in the Y(OH)3 matrix. In addition, the abundant –OH on the surface of Y(OH)3 and CDs can form hydrogen bonds to fix the CDs (Fig. 2f). To provide more evidence that the –OH, –NH2, and –COOH groups on the surface of CDs can form hydrogen bonds with the OH groups in Y(OH)3 (Fig. 3 a), we tested thermogravimetric (TG) curves of CDs@Y(OH)3 and Y(OH)3. As shown in Fig. S3, the TG curves of CDs@Y(OH)3 and Y(OH)3 showed a significant difference. With increasing temperature, CDs@Y(OH)3 exhibited less mass loss compared to Y(OH)3, which is attributed to the stabilizing effect of hydrogen bonds within the composite.

To gain a deeper understanding of the nature of non-covalent interactions between the surface state of CDs and Y(OH)3, we optimized the structures of CDs and Y(OH)3 and calculated their electrostatic potentials (ESPs). As shown in Fig. 3b, the ESP maps reveal that both CDs and Y(OH)3 contain numerous positively charged hydrogen atoms, while the surface of CDs boasts many negatively charged oxygen and nitrogen groups, and Y(OH)3 features negatively charged oxygen atoms. This uneven charge distribution creates conditions conducive to the formation of hydrogen bonds. To further investigate the interactions between Y(OH)3 and CDs, we performed theoretical calculations of the binding energies between the two materials. In Fig. S4, the bond energies between CDs and Y(OH)3 were calculated to be E-HB(a) = −8.01 kcal/mol, E-HB(b) = −3.46 kcal/mol, E-HB(c) = −2.76 kcal/mol, and E-HB(d) = −3.39 kcal/mol, including hydrogen bonds and other weak interactions. Additionally, IGMH visualization analysis was conducted, revealing two distinct isosurfaces between the two materials. The blue region indicated by arrow 1 represents strong interactions, specifically hydrogen bonding interactions, while the green region indicated by arrow 2 is formed by lone electron pairs and Y cations, representing electrostatic interactions (Fig. 3c left). These correspond to peaks 1 and 2 in the δg vs sign(lambda2)rho IGMH scatter plot (Fig. 3c right). Finally, the calculated interaction energy between CDs and Y(OH)3 was −24.26 kcal/mol, which includes contributions from hydrogen bonding and van der Waals attraction. All the above results demonstrate the existence of hydrogen bonding interactions between CDs and Y(OH)3.

Furthermore, the photophysical properties of the CDs and CDs@Y(OH)3 were investigated. The UV–vis absorption and excitation-emission spectra of the CDs@Y(OH)3 are shown in Fig. 4 a. The CDs@Y(OH)3 has an absorption band and an absorption peak at 224–282 nm and 341 nm, which are often ascribed to the π-π∗ transitions of CC in the core of the CDs and n-π∗ transitions of CN/CO bonds, respectively [39]. The UV–vis absorption and phosphorescence excitation spectra have an overlap at 300–385 nm, indicating that the phosphorescence of CDs is related to the surface states. The fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths of the Y(OH)3, CDs, and CDs@Y(OH)3 were also collected (Figs. S5a, S5c, and Fig. 4b). The only extremely weak fluorescence was detected for the pure Y(OH)3. However, with the increase of excitation wavelength, the variation trend of fluorescence emission wavelengths and intensities of CDs@Y(OH)3 was similar to that of CDs, which confirmed from the side that the CDs were incorporated into the Y(OH)3 matrix and the luminescence of the CDs@Y(OH)3 came from the CDs. Additionally, we mechanically mixed the CDs with Y(OH)3 and tested the phosphorescence of the mixture, finding no phosphorescent performance (Fig. S5e), however, we found that CDs@Y(OH)3 produced bright green RTP emission and their RTP emission peak was located at 496 nm (Fig. 4c), However, the RTP of pure CDs and Y(OH)3 were not detected (Figs. S5d and S5b). To further investigate the other RTP properties of CDs@Y(OH)3, we measured the afterglow lifetime of CDs@Y(OH)3, the average lifetime was calculated to be 0.55 s after fitting the decay profile (Fig. 4d). The photoluminescence quantum yield and phosphorescence quantum yield of CDs@Y(OH)3 have been tested, the CDs@Y(OH)3 showed a photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of 20.31 % (Fig. S6a) and a phosphorescence quantum yield (Phos QY) of 8.09 % (Fig. S6b). The above results show that the phosphorescence of CDs was activated by the Y(OH)3 matrix. In Fig. 4e, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of CDs@Y(OH)3 under a 77 K environment were measured and their peak wavelengths were located at 395 nm and 496 nm, respectively. The ΔEST value was calculated to be 0.64 eV (ΔEST = 1240/λFL-1240/λPhos), which is an allowable value for efficient intersystem crossing to generate triplet excitons [40]. Finally, the temperature-dependent afterglow spectra of the CDs@Y(OH)3 were recorded from 77 K to 340 K, as shown in Fig. 4f. The afterglow intensity decreases with rising temperature, and thus this confirms the phosphorescent nature of CDs@Y(OH)3.

High luminescent stability is a key factor for composites to be applied in information security. Therefore, the RTP emission stability of CDs@Y(OH)3 powder was studied. As shown in Fig. S7, the CDs@Y(OH)3 powder was stored in a closed room-temperature environment for over two years. Surprisingly, its phosphorescence had an insignificant change under UV light, indicating that CDs@Y(OH)3 powder had excellent stability. In addition, the phosphorescence stability of 100 mg CDs@Y(OH)3 in different solvents (such as DMF, Ethanol, H2O, DMSO and Methanol) also was measured. The phosphorescent intensity and lifetime of CDs@Y(OH)3 are different (Fig. 5b–d and Fig. 5 c–e), but the phosphorescence phenomenon can still be observed with the naked eye in different environments, as shown in Fig. 5a, due to the protection of the matrix. By isolating the CDs from the external environment, Y(OH)3 not only prevents CDs from being quenched by dissolved oxygen, but also prevents the triplet excited state of the CDs from interacting with the solvent. It's worth noting that the phosphorescence emission of CDs@Y(OH)3 was not observed in an acidic environment (acetic acid, dilute HCl, H3PO4, dilute HNO3 and dilute H2SO4), which can be attributed to the neutralization reaction of OH− in Y(OH)3 with proton in hydrochloric acid. The phosphorescence will be quenched when the CDs lose the protection of the Y(OH)3 matrix, which proves the importance of Y(OH)3 for CDs to produce RTP. The specific phosphorescence emission spectra and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDs@Y(OH)3 under different environments are shown in Figs. S8 and S9.

Moreover, six different kinds of CDs from the previously reported works were randomly selected and prepared, and each was incorporated into the Y(OH)3 matrix. The phosphorescence spectra of six pure CDs are displayed in Figs. S10a–f, they do not have phosphorescence emission. The afterglow spectra and images of CDs-(1–6)@Y(OH)3 are displayed in Figs. S10g–l, Their RTP emission peaks are located at 564 nm, 527 nm, 506 nm, 513 nm, 528 nm and 511 nm due to the difference in CDs species, respectively. These CDs@Y(OH)3 can show green or yellow phosphorescent emission under UV excitation. It proves that the phosphorescence originates from the CDs and Y(OH)3 matrix plays an auxiliary role. At the same time, these results also show that Y(OH)3 is a universal matrix that can stabilize the triplet state of CDs to generate RTP. Moreover, this simple preparation method has the potential to produce CDs based RTP materials on a large scale.

Based on the above results, a possible mechanism for realizing such RTP of CDs@Y(OH)3 with high stability could be clearly explained (Fig. 6 ). The CDs were first incorporated into the Y(OH)3 matrix during the crystallization of Y(OH)3 in a hydrothermal environment. The rigid network of Y(OH)3 can isolate CDs from oxygen and other common solvents, and the hydrogen bonds between Y(OH)3 and CDs can play an important role in confinement on the CDs. More importantly, these factors can limit the intramolecular vibrations and rotations of the CDs and effectively stabilize the triplet excitons of the CDs.

In addition, the CDs are embedded in a rigid matrix, resulting in the excellent stability of CDs@Y(OH)3. The RTP performance of CDs@Y(OH)3 and its acid-reactive properties can be exploited for information encryption and erasure applications. In this system, as shown in Fig. 7 a, the prepared CDs@Y(OH)3 and CDs-1@Y(OH)3 samples are used as encrypted materials, however the prepared Y(OH)3 sample was used as an interference. As shown in Fig. 7b, the information is encrypted into a “9086” pattern using CDs@Y(OH)3 and CDs-1@Y(OH)3. Under the illumination of 365 UV lamps, the error information shows similar fluorescent patterns of “8888,” indistinguishable to the naked eye. When the UV lamp is off, CDs@Y(OH)3 and CDs-1@Y(OH)3 show the “9086” patterns with a green or yellow afterglow, which can be distinguished by the naked eye. Finally, the information can be easily erased with acetic acid spray. Overall, the CDs-based materials have fluorescence, and phosphorescent information encryption models, making them ideal candidates for advanced information protection and encryption technology.

In summary, we report a new CD-based RTP material by incorporating the CDs into Y(OH)3 matrix to achieve efficient phosphorescence emission and excellent stability. The result is attributed to the abundant CO/CN groups of CDs and the confinement effect provided by the Y(OH)3 matrix. The former can produce strong spin-orbit coupling to populate triplet excitons and promote the ISC process, and the latter can well fix CDs through the space constraints imposed by Y(OH)3 on CDs and the formation of hydrogen bonds between Y(OH)3 and CDs. The Y(OH)3 matrix was confirmed to be effective for multiple CDs to help stabilize the triplet states and promote RTP emissions. Benefiting from the protection of a rigid matrix, CDs@Y(OH)3 exhibits excellent stability, which is very favorable for long-term service of materials in information security applications. This work provides a universal route for the synthesis of efficient and stable CD-based RTP materials.

Ping Liang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Yihao Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoyan He: Data curation. Xiaoli Peng: Writing – review & editing. Weilan Huang: Investigation. Beichen Ying: Data curation. Xuejie Zhang: Formal analysis. Chaofan Hu: Investigation. Bingfu Lei: Investigation. Yingliang Liu: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jianle Zhuang: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172142, 12174119), the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2021A0505060007) and the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2022A1515011958, 2024A1515010824).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Color-tunable ultralong organic room temperature phosphorescence from a multicomponent copolymer
Nat. Commun.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient room-temperature phosphorescence of 1D organic-inorganic hybrid metal halides
